item.title: Still top notch!
item.content:encoded:
Eight years later this still is a magnificent work of art .
(In the meantime whole Anatolia turned into an Islamic State.)

item.title: Operational Staff Post
item.content:encoded: Remaining image is Public Domain.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
+1 from me; but, here are some little nitpicks (yes, I know the article is years old)

Fist, the phrase versus the amnestics doesn't balance well — especially if the meme is possibly centuries old. The phrase is easily repeated — amnestics are hard to get. The entire world, including prospective foundation employees, should be dominated by the "existence" type, because it's way too easy to spread. Especially with the advent of radio and television.

Second, the meme is old and of international significance. Why does it have a code phrase in modern colloquial English?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Seven years later and readers are still committedly defending their chosen side. Myself included.

I still so firmly lean to the non-existence side that I clicked on the Wikipedia link up-thread fully expecting a well-crafted phony page. Only fully convinced myself it was a legit entry on realizing that there is no mention of an Islamic Republic there.

This article is a real world meme that exerts, as much as reality permits, the same influence on readers as the fictional memetic anomaly it describes.
Eskobar deserves more kudos than I have to give for this outstanding achievement in metafiction.

There's probably a psychology/sociology paper waiting to be written based on this comment section.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Great article though the location plus 7th century reference makes it fairly clear the not real side are right unless we are dealing with a radically different timeline.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Left me completely mind-boggled. It's fascinating to see the Foundation being split into two (especially the O5s, jesus christ) over an issue where I honestly have no clue which side is correct (though I take it this is completely intentional). +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just wanna say it reminds me of politics. That's what scares me so much about this. Half the people around you have a completely different idea about the world in general, you have to start questioning your own views, but you know doing that won't get you the answer, just what you THINK is the answer. And in the end, can you do anything about it?

The conflict itself that split the foundation in half is the real SCP here, isn't it? This is the intent of this SCP, to make conflict. It's not one or the other. (Or maybe it is… But I don't know.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Why am I flashing back to that one Buzz Lightyear episode where this starfish thing made Starcommand go utterly ballistic and engage in an all out war with itself?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is legitimate scary for me. Like, we're just lucky that this memetic virus only makes us perceive a fictitious nation, but who knows how much worse it could've been? The virus might as well have been something else completely, something lethal or more terrible. And the fact that you don't know who else is infected - until you talk with them, in which case you yourself get infected - means that you live in terror everyday.

That, and also the fact that it could also be the other way - that this "memtic virus" is the real thing, and all the ones that "aren't infected" are the ones with the actual virus.
I love the idea of an SCP that creates a memetic-laden conflicts among people.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"persons affected are completely mentally incapable of seeing that the evidence exists"

That describes most of conspiracy theorists, you know. Both their point of view (I know truth and no one else notices/cares) and themself (theorist will reject any evidence against his conspiracy theory, only question is how poor excuse will be used).

item.title: Added an "in popular culture" section to the wikipedia page for Samothrace
item.content:encoded:
First off, I just want to say that I love this article and its opposing variant, which so perfectly complements it and makes it so much better than it otherwise would be. I would give more praise, but I don't want to end up repeating stuff that people above me have already said, so I'll just keep it at that.

Inspired by one of the comments here, I decided to add a section to the wikipedia page for Samothrace called "In popular culture," to describe this SCP.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I suppose it would be interesting if one iteration had one link and and another had the other.

That is true and equally interesting. I suppose it's a matter of choosing which link for which version.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Right on. Proposal has been updated to change the line of text used in the inline link.

As for your second question, at the current time I was going to add both links to both iterations, though I suppose it would be interesting if one iteration had one link and and another had the other.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I am frankly not entirely on-board with using the term 'Sunni Islam' to be your crosslink point. I mean, ORIA (especially Eskobar's take on it) extends beyond sectarian concerns. Yes, I'm nitpicking the location where links should be, but they are important too.

Considering that you are linking to ORIA's take on IDAS, I think it's better to link it from the mention of "Islamic Republic of Eastern Samothrace". I mean, the article proposed for crosslinking is really about IDAS and not really about Sunni Islam.

Also, do consider to apply all crosslinks to both perspectives featured in this SCP (SCP-1173-A and SCP-1173-1).

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
I mean, both that tale, the GoI doc, and 1173 itself are all written Eskobar, and I see no reason as to why both links can be included since both have very different but equally interesting views about the anomaly.

To that point, including Episiotometrics as a link:

Chaning this:

Individuals can become infected with SCP-1173 only by hearing an operant sentence spoken aloud in their presence by an infected individual; through research, the operant sentence has been determined to be "You hear about that thing down in Samothrace?" Individuals that have heard this sentence frequently express minor interest in the ensuing conversation, which invariably describes some sort of internecine military conflict, massacre, famine, drought, ethnic cleansing, or other atrocity occurring within the country.

To this:

Individuals can become infected with SCP-1173 only by hearing an operant sentence spoken aloud in their presence by an infected individual; through research, the operant sentence has been determined to be "You hear about that thing down in Samothrace?" Individuals that have heard this sentence frequently express minor interest in the ensuing conversation, which invariably describes some sort of internecine military conflict, massacre, famine, drought, ethnic cleansing, or other atrocity occurring within the country.

But, same as above: If anyone else has suggestions, show me what you got.

item.title: Re: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Speaking as part of the WikiWalk team:

I think that there is a better crosslink for this article. For example, this tale.

I don't think the ORIA link is that good. The tale doesn't just have the samothrace reference, it has all of the GoIs references. And the Samothrace reference is also pretty small.

I suggest we use the tale I linked for a crosslink in this article. Where the link itself is I think is up to you.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at anytime.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

So, how bout that thing down in Samothrace? In all seriousness, this is a great SCP and I found coming up with a crosslink difficult, but I think I found something that could work. MEMORANDUM 117 REGARDING PROJECT 025 involves ORIA's take on the Samothrace phenomenon, and since it is the Islamic Union of Eastern Samothrace, a link between here and the ORIA GoI format is not unreasonable, especially if done as a inline link. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

SCP-1173 is a memetic phenomenon centered around a delusional belief in a political entity, known as the "Islamic Union of Eastern Samothrace," "Ισλαμική Δημοκρατία της Ανατολικής Σαμοθράκης" (in the original Greek), or "IDAS." Affected individuals will possess varying degrees of understanding or awareness of the history or culture of the IDAS, but interviews with individuals under the influence of SCP-1173 have isolated several key characteristics that the political entity is commonly described as possessing. Geographically, the IDAS appears to be a primarily Greek-speaking nation comprising a small mainland enclave on the Anatolian peninsula and a series of islands in the northeastern Aegean Sea. As the name would indicate, within the context of the delusion, a significant portion of the population practices Sunni Islam, with a limited number of Shi'a practitioners and a small group of Syriac Orthodox Christians, though the latter group has largely left the country due to religious conflict.

To this:

SCP-1173 is a memetic phenomenon centered around a delusional belief in a political entity, known as the "Islamic Union of Eastern Samothrace," "Ισλαμική Δημοκρατία της Ανατολικής Σαμοθράκης" (in the original Greek), or "IDAS." Affected individuals will possess varying degrees of understanding or awareness of the history or culture of the IDAS, but interviews with individuals under the influence of SCP-1173 have isolated several key characteristics that the political entity is commonly described as possessing. Geographically, the IDAS appears to be a primarily Greek-speaking nation comprising a small mainland enclave on the Anatolian peninsula and a series of islands in the northeastern Aegean Sea. As the name would indicate, within the context of the delusion, a significant portion of the population practices Sunni Islam, with a limited number of Shi'a practitioners and a small group of Syriac Orthodox Christians, though the latter group has largely left the country due to religious conflict.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Just wanted to tell that this (maybe along with that thing that steals words speaking about it) is my most favourite SCP ever.
Glad to be the 400th upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
We only are able to understand the world through the lens of perception. Either Samothrace exists and we are infected with the meme that makes us think it doesn't, in which case we edit out all information about it on our own (if you go there you just believe the island is part of Greece); or it doesn't exist but some people are infected with the meme of that it does, in which case they imagine and remember information about it that is totally fabricated — if they go to the island they imagine it is independent.

Unless, of course Samothrace represents the merger of two otherwise alternate realities, and isn't truly memetic at all, in which case people in the Samothrace negative reality travel to the Greek island and Samothrace positive people travel to the Republic of Samothrace. If the two go together they literally would perceive different realities and would only be able to confuse and frustrate each other.

The thing is that it is impossible for an observer to differetiate between these possibilities. You either perceive Islamic Samothrace or you do not. You perceive that some other people claim to perceive the opposite. This would be the case if you are infected with an anomalous meme and it would be true if they were and it would be true if you were from alternate universes; but there is no way to tell which of these is objectively true.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's a great thought experiment in SCP format. That said, what happens to citizens then? Modern media make viral clickbait for views almost every second of the day it feels like. Are we assuming the Foundation just censors all media on Samothrace even when people who contend it exists are in the Foundation?

Wouldn't this lead to further thought experiments of spreading to different societies that would invariable lead to the questioning of Samothrace's very existence which would then lead to research teams going to that designated area to research it?

It would become a VERY real Schrodinger's cat for the entire world. Sure one can argue the Foundation has contained many things of even larger significance, but short of a world-wide amnesiac vaccination, there will still be a lot of people who confirm or deny it's existence once media censors have been implemented. It seems that regardless of which binary decision, it remains as a "thing", an after-thought, inside of their memory.

So we could have media that is either influenced directly by the anomalous item with no proxy.

Or through a proxy human who was influenced (either binary decision) and will create articles. Of which debate will be sparked.

Don't… necessarily know how this hasn't ended the world at this point, but maybe I'm overthinking this.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Do we meet notability standards?

ETA:. We have an article so I guess we do?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It is so tempting to put an "In popular culture" section on that Wikipedia article with a link to here…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since we all know Samothrace doesn't exist

an SCP that disproves itself really the kills suspension of disbelief

Well, I suppose we'll just have to agree to disagree. But hey, did you hear about that thing down in Samothrace?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ok this is one of the few times Ive been legit mindfucked by this site. I actually read the other iteration first (which I thouroughly enjoyed), not even knowing this version existed. Imagine my confusion when reading this.

Hmm, wasnt there an image of an impoverished boy instead of a map?

Hmm, wasnt it the Islamic Republic of Samothrace?

Why do the effects seem different?

(Goes to check edit history) nothing…omg Im going mad :(

Oh wait…forgot to click that link

*explosion*

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea in theory, but the notion that it would create this massive foundation-spanning shadow war even between the O5s seems kinda dubious. Smells of political allegory, especially with such a politically charged topic as a nebulous "conflict in the middle east."

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I love everything about this SCP. Not only is the SCP itself thought provoking, the prospect of a foundation covert civil war is really nice.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The fact that I've formed a very strong opinion about which of these is true in-universe is testament to the bit of human psychology this skip addresses. Take my +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
343 upvotes?

GOD IS ON THIS SCPS SIDE HALLELUJAH!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Woah…
Great concept of doubt taken to the next level.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of my favorite articles on the site, I think it is great how it could be viewed as a commentary on people in the real world ignoring issues taking place around the world, because it does not affect them directly.

What is actually happening within the context of the article? I like the ambiguity, but I will point out that "Samothrace is real" is more terrifying than the alternative, since it means that strife is occurring in this country, but no matter what happens, the world at large is not capable of even acknowledging this entire country, or anyone who has even been there.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is one of my all time favorites!

It starts out as a very solid memetic SCP that has a very high believability factor. No monsters, only very light on the physics bending and still a huge implied horror of an ethnic cleansing being covered up.

And then it hits you with the meta… this SCP is not containable at all! The fact that the Foundation knows about is turning out to be an actual threat to the stability of the Foundation.

I love the concept of information that is bad for you, and I find this to be a very pleasant example for this. Would upvote again!

item.title: Re: + 300
item.content:encoded: Someone get Doc Wheeler in here! We've got an antimeme!
Hey, you hear about that thing down in Samothrace?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Heck, why else does O5-1 not assent to either side?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Now that I think of it, the article would be much better, at least in the eyes of the geographically well read, to make it Thrace instead of Samothrace. One, the 'small section of Anatolia' described is very close to Thrace; Two, Samothrace is a small island with a population of c.1000 people; Three, Samothrace is Greek territory, and pretty much exclusively Greek Orthodox.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This was just brilliant. There are a few SCPs on this site that really just stand head and shoulders above others, and I believe this is one of them. Absolutely fantastic.

I really am not sure which side is correct. On the one hand, the "belief" meme spreads more like previously cataloged memes, spreading by exposure to a memetic trigger (a picture, idea, or in this case, the phrase). On the other hand, the "non-belief" meme affects individuals more severely, causing violent outbursts when confronted with contradictory evidence (assaulting christian refugees while shouting "You can't be real!"), therefore acting more like a meme (thing what makes you go crazy). Really, I just think the whole article was genius.

(I do like Major Blackwood's suggestion about blackboxing the name of the country, but think the article is still an amazing work of literature without it.)

One thing I'd like to add to the discussion though, I don't think that the hostility between believers and non-believers is part of the SCP. It's an interesting idea, but one I don't think has any teeth in the article.

The conflict is definitely a Foundation thing, with its imperative to maintain normalcy at all costs. The precise nature of the conflict is not anger over what the other side believes or doesn't, it's about what needs to be Contained. The believers aren't pissed off that the non-believers are ignoring the Samothracian tragedies. They're just trying to contain a memetic hazard. And if the hostility was part of the SCP, then the entire world would be at war with itself. With no Protocol Omicron in place to turn it into a secret war, Joe Smoe and John Doe would be getting into fistfights on Mainstreet, Everywhere.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
…yes?

Now my brain hurts, again. It only exists if you're looking at it (whether the cat is alive or dead can't be known until you view the cat), but that's actually the case here. If you're seeing evidence of Samothrace than you're infected with the belief it's real, because the uninfected ignore evidence it doesn't exist and thus, I assume, can't be looking at it. But if you aren't looking at it, you're infected with the belief that it doesn't exist, since the uninfected see proof it exists all over the place.

And I thought I'd gotten my head around the thing down in Samothrace!

Proof my brain is weird, the following train of thought just happened: [pondered how to prove who's right], screw it, my head hurts, I'm gonna go watch my tortoise, let him decide who's right, oh wait, that jade tortoise scip would know wouldn't it?

Though, given the apparent risk of violence from those being told they're wrong, that'd probably just end in a broken jade tortoise when someone threw it at a wall.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"This message not to be kept in official records.

This message not to be copied or replicated."

Two missing "Is" es.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Is this Schrödinger's Islamic State?
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I disagree with this.

If Samothrace exists, and the "Samothrace doesn't exist" meme wins out, the result will be a humanitarian catastrophe. Trade and aid will both stop.

If Samothrace doesn't exist, and the "Samothrace does exist" meme wins out, the result will be a great deal of wasted aid and trade, and a tremendous amount of attention paid to a country which doesn't exist that would be better spent elsewhere.

Either way, a lot of people could die because they don't get the resources they need.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ugh, that hurt my head.

Who is right? Is Eastern Samothrace a phantom city trying to be remembered? Is some wealthy shadow government attempting to hide the atrocities hidden within? Is nobody right, and this is just the first step of an even bigger memetic contagion designed to wipe out the Earth?

Take your +1 and get out, everything's starting to get all fuzzy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was about to complain that it's not linking to the right version of the page (starting with the map-pic one)

Then I realized it randomly selects a version to display upon viewing of the standard link scp-wiki.net/SCP-1173

Holy fucking fawk meta as fawk

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually got the "Samothrace doesn't exist" article when I first opened the page, but I put off reading it, and due to a forced reload in the interim, I got the other, and I noticed that the picture had changed, so I refreshed and it went back to the first one, and I thought it must somehow be like that picture that changes depending on who's looking at it. I read the article…and then I read the second addendum and thought to myself, "Wow, talk about anomalous overreaction…whoever wrote this must have been infected and part of the infection is violence towards uninfected people coupled with believing all uninfected people to be infected - like that printer with a soul, sort of." Then I started reading the discussion page, and I saw some people talk about the article with the kid on it and talking about it like it was a different thing, so I went back and refreshed a couple of times to see if I'd get the other article, but I didn't, but then I noticed the article mentioned in the containment procedures, which the second addendum had blown me away so far that I'd forgotten about it.

So now that I've gone back, read both articles and all three discussion sections, and am past half an hour late to a vital sleep hygiene ritual of mine so that I'll be falling asleep way late tonight and my mom will be furious with me, I can only say…um…I don't know. I just don't know. I know I was uninfected before reading this article, because I was aware of neither the country nor the adamant denial of the existence of the country, but now I must be infected by something…maybe two things. What really strikes me - what still strikes me, even now that I have read both articles - is the anomalous degree of hostility the two sides have towards each other. Someone sort of brought this up earlier, but the whole "God help you." line in both second addenda, and the degree of urgency to the "uninfect people!!!" sentiment, seems more anomalous than the country itself, whether said country exists or not. Maybe the hostility is the anomaly, in which case O5-1 is the only one even trying to contain it. Which is scary.

+1, I can't do anything else, but…it's more of a "I literally don't know what to think" upvote than a "Holy fuck, my mind, it has been blown" upvote, which is a much less common type of upvote for me to give - most of the time, if I literally don't know what to think, it's a novote or sometimes a downvote. So I guess that makes this article impressive…maybe? I DON'T KNOW!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll bet Nobody knows for sure, Nobody always seems to know.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
While the basic idea about the two articles were brilliant, I can't help but notice: the anomaly is actually not that scary… at least not scary enough to warrant some sort of civil war.
Its not like, just because half the world can/can't remember a country, we have to fight about it. This is really pretty minor compared to some other stuff the foundation deals with.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Confused about what? That thing down in Samothrace?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I am incredibly confused right now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: One of my favorite SCPs on this site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Oh god I love this one. I was expecting it to turn into a tract about how the world ignores bad things that happen in small, poor countries, and aren't we horrible. But it became so much more than that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just… Bravo. Bravo.

Edit: welp, didn't intend for this to be a nestled reply, but apparently I can't delete it. Auspicious first comment.

I blame my phone.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It doesn't disprove itself.
Oh, you think you know Samothrace doesn't exist? Right, you're certain of that?

But Samothrace DOES exist. You just don't know that because you're infected.

Or maybe I'm wrong, and Samothrace doesn't exist, but the "Samothrace does exist" meme does exist.

Your position is exactly the same as that of the in-universe non-believers. You don't believe Samothrace exists. How does that make this SCP break SoD?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Excellent article, and brilliant execution. Every possible way of reconciling whether this place does or does not exist is subject to tampering by the other side, and you provide ample evidence supporting each point of view.

The implications of either party (or both parties) being correct are broad, too. Either someone is interested in spreading a false message and spreading civil strife around the world, or someone has already successfully suppressed all knowledge about an entire nation-state and its people.

So good. This is one of the best-crafted articles I've read. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I hadn't thought of it that way. I guess that makes sense, excessive blackboxing annoys me, too. My focus was mostly just on suspension of disbelief.

Props for being calm and mature about your opinion, good sir.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Okay, fair enough, suspension of disbelief is a valid point and I do apologize for the possible connotations of my previous comment.

But, you will notice, that it's not all information about Samothrace that triggers an infection, it's a specific phrase that does. Or maybe it's the Class O Amnestic. Either way, the conflict between Foundation coworkers is more of the point of the article than the actual (possible?) location itself, and redacting the name of it really wouldn't do anything.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not trying to be a smartass. I'm just talking about suspension of disbelief.. Of course SCPs aren't real. But an SCP that disproves itself really the kills suspension of disbelief. I thought people on this site were a fan of SCPs that hinted" at being real, like the article on the box that mentions you by your wikidot name.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I… :|

Please tell me you're being smart with me right now.

Of course the Samothrace doesn't actually exist. I mean, that's obvious and shouldn't even really need to be stated. However, in-universe, the point is they don't actually know whether or not it is an actually country with memetic effects. Is it fake? Is it real?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Because otherwise I wouldn't remember this SCP at all. Passive resistance and all that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Neither do I. Merely redacting the name "Samothrace" wouldn't be enough to achieve such effect and even if other changes were made in order to do so, I find it hard to see why do we need to make changes to an article that is not only highly acclaimed critically, but also implies that the SCP in which it's based on could be the most widespread anomalous infection in the entire Foundation’s universe.
